title: From Ease To Difficulty
study: A Study In The Life Of Joseph
scriptureForWeek: GENESIS 41:33-57 "Therefore, Pharaoh should find an intelligent and wise man and put him in charge of the entire land of Egypt. Then Pharaoh should appoint supervisors over the land and let them collect one-fifth of all the crops during the seven good years. Have them gather all the food produced in the good years that are just ahead and bring it to Pharaoh’s storehouses. Store it away, and guard it so there will be food in the cities. That way there will be enough to eat when the seven years of famine come to the land of Egypt. Otherwise this famine will destroy the land.” Joseph’s suggestions were well received by Pharaoh and his officials. So Pharaoh asked his officials, “Can we find anyone else like this man so obviously filled with the spirit of God?” Then Pharaoh said to Joseph, “Since God has revealed the meaning of the dreams to you, clearly no one else is as intelligent or wise as you are. You will be in charge of my court, and all my people will take orders from you. Only I, sitting on my throne, will have a rank higher than yours.” Pharaoh said to Joseph, “I hereby put you in charge of the entire land of Egypt.” Then Pharaoh removed his signet ring from his hand and placed it on Joseph’s finger. He dressed him in fine linen clothing and hung a gold chain around his neck. Then he had Joseph ride in the chariot reserved for his second-in-command. And wherever Joseph went, the command was shouted, “Kneel down!” So Pharaoh put Joseph in charge of all Egypt. And Pharaoh said to him, “I am Pharaoh, but no one will lift a hand or foot in the entire land of Egypt without your approval.” Then Pharaoh gave Joseph a new Egyptian name, Zaphenath-paneah. He also gave him a wife, whose name was Asenath. She was the daughter of Potiphera, the priest of On. So Joseph took charge of the entire land of Egypt. He was thirty years old when he began serving in the court of Pharaoh, the king of Egypt. And when Joseph left Pharaoh’s presence, he inspected the entire land of Egypt.
weekTheme: Boys avoid, men accept This week we are looking at the difficulty of responsibility. Learning to accept responsibility and handle the difficulties that come with it is a key element of growing into a godly man in the world. Extended adolescence in today’s society keeps young men focused on themselves, living in a selfish state with a “me- centered” reality. We need men with an “other-centered” reality willing to own and handle personal leadership, and men who can be counted on. An irresponsible man is a burden to the world; a responsible man is a gift. This week, we learn how Joseph handled responsibility to help others.

days.title: The Difficulty Of Responsibility
days.scripture: GENESIS 41:33-36 33 “Therefore, Pharaoh should find an intelligent and wise man and put him in charge of the entire land of Egypt. 34 Then Pharaoh should appoint supervisors over the land and let them collect one-fifth of all the crops during the seven good years. 35 Have them gather all the food produced in the good years that are just ahead and bring it to Pharaoh’s storehouses. Store it away, and guard it so there will be food in the cities. 36 That way there will be enough to eat when the seven years of famine come to the land of Egypt. Otherwise this famine will destroy the land.”
days.reflection: At last! Things are finally turning around for Joseph. He is taken from prison and put before Pharaoh. Pharaoh has recognized Joseph's intelligence and wisdom, and he is about to be set free. But something strange happens - different than what you may expect. Joseph isn’t released to go home and get his coat and resume his former life. Joseph is promoted to running the whole country of Egypt. His faithfulness doesn’t get him a vacation; his faithfulness gets him a job. Look at the words used in the verses above about what Joseph is going to do: Supervise. Prepare. Gather. Store. Guard. These are strong words. These are powerful words. These are not words used when dealing with children. These are words used to describe what men do. This is important to understand in terms of growing up and becoming a man. You may think the older you get, the less you have to do, but the truth is the older you get, the more you get to do. Men are designed by God to be responsible and to handle hard things. They are made to be responsible for themselves, for those they love, and for those in their community. Men accept responsibility; boys try and shrink out of it. In our world today, there is a thing called extended adolescence. It means that the period from becoming a teenager to becoming a man has gotten longer and longer and longer. The path of growth used to look like this: Baby. Toddler. Child. Teenager. Adult. But now it looks like this: Baby. Toddler. Child. Teenager. ______________________ Adult. That big gap means teenagers get stuck in this long funky period where they fail to mature. They are not boys, but they aren’t men either. They live like they are in high school when they are in their twenties. These people are called man- agers. They are as old as men but still live like teenagers. You do not want to be a man-ager. Boys get to play all the time, not men. Boys get to live off their parent's money, not men. Boys get to be pushed around in strollers, not men. Boys get to open their presents first, not men. Being a child is wonderful. It’s a magical time. But being childish when you should be a man is not magical. It’s kind of sad. Although it's hard and can even be painful, learning to accept responsibility and grow up is great and totally worth the work. Because the rewards of accepting responsibility are incredible. Men get to drive cars, not boys. Men get to be married, not boys. Men get to have kids, not boys. Men get to be bosses, not boys. This may seem a bit intense, but there is no need to stress out. You are off to a great start in becoming a responsible man. You took the time to read this, didn’t you? Just keep going and keep growing. Joseph did and he ended up in a position of unbelievable responsibility and reward. Accept each challenge and test as it comes. Embrace the difficulties of responsibility and you will be amazed at what God will trust you with.
questions: When you hear the words responsible and responsibility, how do these words make you feel? Do they make you excited or nervous? Why?
Can you think of some examples of men acting like man-agers today?
Is there a couple of ways you can be a bit more responsible in your life right now? What might they be?

thoughts.verse: When I was a child, I spoke and thought and reasoned as a child. But when I grew up, I put away childish things.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to help you continue to grow and mature. Ask him to give you a vision of being a mature and godly man.

days.title: The Rewards Of Faithfulness
days.scripture: GENESIS 41:39-40 39 Then Pharaoh said to Joseph, “Since God has revealed the meaning of the dreams to you, clearly no one else is as intelligent or wise as you are. 40 You will be in charge of my court, and all my people will take orders from you. Only I, sitting on my throne, will have a rank higher than yours.”
days.reflection: Sometimes when you are young, you can think that small things don’t matter. You can believe that you don’t need to focus on little details when there are much bigger things to do. Why worry about something as small as cleaning your room when you have a big exam? Why worry about doing the dishes or helping your parents with chores around the house when your friends are asking you to go to a game at school? In some sense, this is understandable because you don’t care about doing the stuff adults have to worry about, but in other ways, it’s a trap. How you do the small things tells those around you if you can be trusted in bigger things. Jesus said in Luke 16:10-12: “If you are faithful in little things, you will be faithful in large ones. But if you are dishonest in little things, you won’t be honest with greater responsibilities. And if you are untrustworthy about worldly wealth, who will trust you with the true riches of heaven? And if you are not faithful with other people’s things, why should you be trusted with things of your own?” Look at this principle in Josephs's life. He is now being given a HUGE amount of power from Pharaoh and is the second most important person in the whole land. He has gone from the prison to the palace in one day! Wild! But it didn’t happen because he had a big plan or because he wanted to be in the Egyptian government. It happened because he was faithful in the small things. God elevated him because he could trust him. Before he helped Pharaoh, he helped Potiphar. He served as a slave before he served as a leader. Before he helped Pharaoh, he helped the captain of the prison. He made sure that things ran as well as they could, even though he hated being there. Before he helped Pharaoh, he helped the people who worked for Pharaoh. He interpreted dreams for people because they were anxious, and he knew God had given him a gift. He learned to be faithful when no one was looking. Therefore, God knew if he was given more power, he would use it well. And Pharaoh realized that God was with him and that he could be trusted. If you had something that mattered to you and your friend asked to borrow it, but then they wrecked it, would you give them something more valuable to you? NO! You would know they couldn’t be trusted. But if you lent them something and they gave it back better than you gave it to them, you would be open to giving them more. Joseph was faithful in little, and so he was given more. If you learn to be faithful, the same will happen to you. There is no shortcut to freedom and privilege; you have to walk the path of faithfulness. Start walking that path today, and you have no idea how far it will take you or where it will lead in the years to come. Small things lead to big things.
questions: If you had to evaluate yourself on how trustworthy you are on a scale of 1-10, how would you rate yourself? Why?
Can you give an example of a time that you were faithful in something that seems pretty small? What was it, and how did it make you feel to know you did something well?
Do you think people your age today understand the importance of faithfulness? Why or why not?

thoughts.verse: The trustworthy person will get a rich reward, but a person who wants quick riches will get into trouble.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to thank God that he is faithful to you. Ask him to help you learn to value being faithful and to give you the power to do it.

days.title: Reputation And Responsibility
days.scripture: GENESIS 41:37-40 37 Joseph’s suggestions were well received by Pharaoh and his officials. 38 So Pharaoh asked his officials, “Can we find anyone else like this man so obviously filled with the spirit of God?” 39 Then Pharaoh said to Joseph, “Since God has revealed the meaning of the dreams to you, clearly no one else is as intelligent or wise as you are. 40 You will be in charge of my court, and all my people will take orders from you. Only I, sitting on my throne, will have a rank higher than yours.”
days.reflection: What is your reputation? Have you ever thought about that? What’s your reputation with your friends? What’s your reputation with your teachers? What’s your reputation with your family? The dictionary defines a reputation as “recognition by other people of some characteristic or ability, a place in public esteem or regard: a good name.” Your reputation will determine how much responsibility you will be given in life. That’s why Proverbs 22:1 says, “Choose a good reputation over great riches; being held in high esteem is better than silver or gold.” Ever since Joseph was sold into slavery, he worked hard to have a good reputation. He showed characteristics of integrity, hard work, reliability, and trust in every situation he faced. He embodied the verse above. His reputation was worth more than most of the money in Egypt, even though he had no power or position. Pharaoh could recognize that. Even though Joseph was pulled out of prison, he was immediately elevated because of his reputation of wisdom and divine favor that could be seen by anyone around him. Your reputation is going to end up defining your life too. It will impact the way God uses you. It will impact how girls view you when you want to date. It will impact the way people think about you when you get a job. It will impact the college you are selected to enter if you choose to go. A good reputation will get you massive responsibility which can lead to massive rewards. To build a good reputation: Be consistent. If you are known as someone who does the right thing day after day, people will know you as someone they can count on. Remember -small things lead to big things if you get a reputation that you can be trusted. Be proactive. Don’t just wait for things to happen. Take action. If the trash needs to be taken out, take it out. If your homework needs to be done, do it. A proactive teenager is on the fast track to manhood. Hold to your convictions. Don’t compromise on what you believe. Don’t be a people pleaser who changes your opinion to fit in with other folks, or people will believe you have no integrity. You can start to build a good and godly reputation right now! You don’t have to wait until you are older. Paul wrote to Timothy, “Don’t let anyone think less of you because you are young. Be an example to all believers in what you say, in the way you live, in your love, your faith, and your purity.” (1 Timothy 4:12) Josephs's reputation took him from the prison to the palace in a day. God’s kindness made it possible. A good reputation can take you to amazing places too.
questions: If you could describe yourself in 3 words, what would they be?
Ask the person you are doing this study with what their reputation was when they were your age. Ask them how that impacted how others treated them.
Why do you think Christians seem to have a bad reputation in our world today? How does that make you feel?

thoughts.verse: My child, never forget the things I have taught you. Store my commands in your heart. If you do this, you will live many years, and your life will be satisfying. Never let loyalty and kindness leave you! Tie them around your neck as a reminder. Write them deep within your heart. Then you will find favor with both God and people, and you will earn a good reputation.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to give you a desire for a good reputation. Ask him for forgiveness and help if there are some ways you have dishonored or displeased him.

days.title: The Disaster Of Rejecting Responsibility
days.scripture: GENESIS 41:33-36 33 “Therefore, Pharaoh should find an intelligent and wise man and put him in charge of the entire land of Egypt. 34 Then Pharaoh should appoint supervisors over the land and let them collect one- fifth of all the crops during the seven good years. 35 Have them gather all the food produced in the good years that are just ahead and bring it to Pharaoh’s storehouses. Store it away, and guard it so there will be food in the cities. 36 That way there will be enough to eat when the seven years of famine come to the land of Egypt. Otherwise, this famine will destroy the land.”
days.reflection: What happens if you fail to accept responsibility? Is it really that bad? What’s wrong with being a bit immature or letting things slide? Does everyone really have to be so uptight about growing up? What’s wrong with a few years of taking it easy, pushing off adulthood? Having fun? The answer is clear. Disaster. Pharaoh put Joseph in power so that “the famine would not destroy the land.” If Joseph refused to use his gifts and talents the way God commanded him, many people would have been deeply affected. Thousand would have died. God did not deliver Joseph so he could take it easy; God delivered Joseph so he could take responsibility. God is always looking for people willing to help and change the way things are; these are the people he will use to change the world. You may think the stakes are not as high for you - you’re just a young man, trying to get through school, have friends, and figure out what to do next summer. But you have WAY more potential than that. God has called you for a purpose. He wants to use you to help other people, stop bad things from happening, show others love, and make a real difference. This will all start to happen when you start accepting responsibility for your life. The first man, Adam, refused to accept responsibility for what went wrong in his world. He didn’t accept responsibility for sin. He blamed everything on his wife Eve. He pointed the finger at Satan. He had a victim mentality that things were happening and he could do nothing about them. But he could have. He could have yelled at Satan. He could have challenged his wife; he could have obeyed God. Adam's failure to accept responsibility led to the horror and heartache we still feel today. It’s called sin and the fall. Many men do the same thing today. They live entitled lives, thinking only of themselves and what they want. When things go wrong, it's always someone’s fault. Blame, blame, blame. You do not want to be like that. You want to be like Joseph, but even more, you want to be like Jesus. Jesus never sinned, yet he came into the world to save sinners. He took responsibility for our sin, even though it wasn’t his fault. He didn’t blame; he loved. He didn’t complain; he served. He didn’t shrink back; he rose up. Jesus dealt with sin and brought love, healing, and hope. He bought the kingdom of God into the world. Responsibility can be remembered like this: Response – ability. You have the ability to choose how you respond in any situation. Respond like Jesus, not like Adam. Accepting responsibility leads to disaster or destiny. God used Joseph to save a nation, and he will use you to do some really cool stuff too! If you are willing to step up.
questions: Why do you think so many young people refuse to accept responsibility and blame others when things go wrong these days?
If you were in the garden with Adam and Eve, what would you say to them when Satan tempted them? What advice or warning would you give?
Ask the person you are doing this study with to share a time when they accepted reasonability and refused to blame others. What happened, and how did it affect their life?

thoughts.verse: You have heard me teach things that have been confirmed by many reliable witnesses. Now teach these truths to other trustworthy people who will be able to pass them on to others.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to ask God to help you embrace the gift of responsibility and be like Jesus today. Pay attention today and see if he doesn’t give you the chance.

days.title: The Rewards Of Responsibility
days.scripture: GENESIS 41:45-52 45 Then Pharaoh gave Joseph a new Egyptian name, Zaphenath-paneah. He also gave him a wife, whose name was Asenath. She was the daughter of Potiphera, the priest of On. So Joseph took charge of the entire land of Egypt. 46 He was thirty years old when he began serving in the court of Pharaoh, the king of Egypt. And when Joseph left Pharaoh’s presence, he inspected the entire land of Egypt. 50 During this time, before the first of the famine years, two sons were born to Joseph and his wife, Asenath, the daughter of Potiphera, the priest of On. 51 Joseph named his older son Manasseh, for he said, “God has made me forget all my troubles and everyone in my father’s family.” 52 Joseph named his second son Ephraim, for he said, “God has made me fruitful in this land of my grief.”
days.reflection: This week you have focused on the difficulty of responsibility. For many guys your age, this isn’t a super popular topic. It can feel more like a beatdown or burden. Maybe there is even a question in your heart regarding all this. Maybe the question is something like this… Is it worth it? Is it worth the struggle to begin to put away childish things? Is it worth saying no to some fun stuff to become more consistent and trustworthy? Is it worth taking action instead of sitting around and being told what to do? Is it worth following Jesus and taking your faith seriously? The answer is YES! You have to understand that God doesn’t just want you to be responsible; he rewards you for being responsible. When Joseph steps up, he is blessed. He is put in charge of the entire land of Egypt. He is given a wife, resources, and ends up with children. Even though he was sold into slavery, lied about, betrayed, and forgotten, he never lost heart. He was responsible in Potiphar’s house, and it led to his promotion. He was responsible in prison, and it led to his promotion. And now he is going to be rewarded for accepting responsibility to lead the land. A lot of walking with God includes rewards. It doesn’t mean things will always be easy, but it does mean it will be worth it. Look at what Jesus says in Matthew 19:29: "And everyone who has given up houses or brothers or sisters or father or mother or children or property, for my sake, will receive a hundred times as much in return and will inherit eternal life." And Hebrews 10:3 says, “So do not throw away this confident trust in the Lord. Remember the great reward it brings you!” Don’t throw away the progress you are making into manhood just because it gets hard. Don’t throw away your trust in God because other kids make fun of your faith. Don’t throw away your convictions just to fit in. God himself will reward you for your responsibility and faith. Keep doing these studies. Keep reading God's word. Keep growing. You will love the person God will make you as you grow. When things get tough or you are tempted to drop back to immature and selfish behavior, remember to lift your eyes beyond the moment to the rewards ahead. That’s how Jesus got through the horror of the cross. Hebrews 12:1-3 says, “And let us run with endurance the race God has set before us. We do this by keeping our eyes on Jesus, the champion who initiates and perfects our faith. Because of the joy awaiting him, he endured the cross, disregarding its shame. Now he is seated in the place of honor beside God’s throne. Think of all the hostility he endured from sinful people; then you won’t become weary and give up.” Don’t grow weary; don’t lose heart. Keep moving forward. God rewarded Joseph, and he will reward you too.
questions: What is one of your favorite rewards you received for doing something well?
If you could pick any reward for doing these Primal Path studies, what would it be?
What is one reward you are looking forward to when you become an adult?

thoughts.verse: And it is impossible to please God without faith. Anyone who wants to come to him must believe that God exists and that he rewards those who sincerely seek him.
prompts: Is there something that stands out to you in it?
Is there a question you have about any of the words and what they mean?
Do you think you could explain what it means to someone else if they asked?

days.prayer: Take a moment to thank God for the responsibility Jesus showed to take away our sin and give us eternal life. Ask him to give you a vision like he had, and to help set some joy before you for whatever struggle you have right now.

extras: WEEK 7 MANSCHOOL DINNER FOR 2 MANSCHOOL REMINDER: "The recipe for fun is pretty simple when you’re raising boys: add to any activity an element of danger, stir in a little exploration, add a dash of destruction, and you’ve got yourself a winner." John Eldredge OPTION 1. DINNER FOR 2 Ask your son to cook dinner for you. Let him pick the meal. Make him responsible for getting the ingredients, preparing the meal, and cleaning up afterward. Remind him that at a later point in his life he is going to have to accept responsibility and do this every night. Thank him for the meal and ask him what he learned from the experience. OPTION 2. WATCH Watch The Pursuit of Happyness. This is a great reminder of the refusal to be a victim and accept responsibility to build a life that matters. Great story and acting with Will Smith at his best (pre-slap too ☺). Also shows the rewards that come from accepting responsibility. OPTION 3. DINNER AND DISCUSSION Go to one of your son's favorite restaurants and recap the week. Share from your own story the struggles that you have faced in learning to accept responsibility when you were his age. Try and tell some stories of how you learned to embrace hard things and put away childish things. Ask him if there is anything he is struggling to let go of to grow up. There is some good stuff in this article about teaching your kids responsibility too.
FIELD NOTES PRAYER FOR MY SON
